summary: Review #25. All nine items from review #24 were addressed — this is the best single-cycle improvement streak we've seen. Character components are now memo'd (both CharacterAvatar and Character), constants are cleanly organized across constants.js and quotes.js, the LA timezone hardcoding is gone from both App.jsx AND Office.jsx, the card-flip info button tabIndex is fixed, the vibe streak has aria-label, and the duplicate tooltip CSS block is gone. Exceptional responsiveness from the team. However, the whiteboard fix introduced a new bug: font-size:0 hides the text but the ::before pseudo-element has no content property, leaving a blank white box at 480px. Additionally, App.jsx has grown to 31KB with 10+ colocated component functions — architectural debt that will compound. Role='img' on the interactive office floor is an ARIA semantic conflict. Score holds at 3.8 pending the whiteboard regression fix.

suggestions.title: Whiteboard shows blank white box at 480px — ::before has no content
suggestions.description: Office.css, @media (max-width: 480px): The fix for whiteboard text wrapping used font-size:0 to hide the rendered text, then added .office-whiteboard::before { font-size: 0.7rem } expecting a pseudo-element to show a fallback. But there is NO content property on .office-whiteboard::before anywhere in the file. At 480px the whiteboard renders as a blank white rectangle. Fix: add content: attr(aria-label) to the ::before rule so it pulls the emoji+text from the JSX aria-label, OR simpler: keep font-size:0 on the text but use overflow-wrap:break-word + a smaller font-size (0.36rem) instead. The one-liner: .office-whiteboard::before { content: attr(aria-label); font-size: 0.55rem; }

suggestions.title: office-floor has role='img' but contains focusable tabIndex=0 children
suggestions.description: Office.jsx line 495: <div className='office-floor' role='img' aria-label='Virtual office visualization...'> — then inside it, every Character component renders with tabIndex={0} (line 363). The ARIA spec states that elements with role='img' are presentational containers: their accessible children are suppressed and interactive descendants are not expected. Keyboard users can tab into the characters inside a container that announces itself as a static image — a semantic contradiction that confuses screen readers and misrepresents the interactivity model. Fix: change role='img' to role='region' (landmark) and keep the aria-label. This correctly declares a navigable region containing interactive focusable characters. One-word change.

suggestions.title: App.jsx is 31KB with 10+ component functions — needs splitting
suggestions.description: App.jsx currently contains: ActivityLogEntry, ActivityLog, StatusBadge, ProgressBar, WorkerCard, DeltaBadge, StatsBar, EmptyState, LoadingSkeleton, and the default App export — all in one 31KB file. Office.jsx is 27KB containing CharacterAvatar (15+ named character SVGs), Character, WindowElement, and Office. This is unsustainable for maintenance, code review, and tree-shaking. Suggested split: src/components/WorkerCard.jsx, src/components/ActivityLog.jsx, src/components/StatsBar.jsx, src/components/SkeletonState.jsx. Immediate wins: faster PRs, easier unit testing, better HMR performance in dev. This is medium effort but becomes large effort the longer it's deferred.

suggestions.title: CSS data-tooltip not accessible to screen readers
suggestions.description: Office.css lines 461-481: character tooltips use .char[data-tooltip]::after { content: attr(data-tooltip) } — a CSS-only pseudo-element tooltip. Screen readers do not read CSS-generated content. A keyboard user navigating to a working character in the office will hear the character's aria-label (from CharacterAvatar's aria-label) but NOT the task tooltip (e.g. 'Fixing login page CSS'). Fix: in Character's JSX, add title={tooltip} alongside the existing data-tooltip attribute. title is read by screen readers as a fallback accessible name and also shows as a native browser tooltip on hover. One attribute addition, zero CSS changes.

suggestions.title: ActivityLog reverses entries array inline on every render — missing useMemo
suggestions.description: App.jsx ActivityLog component: const displayed = [...entries].reverse().slice(0, 20) — this creates a new array on EVERY render. ActivityLog re-renders on every 60-second tick (useTimeTick) and on every filter change. For 20 entries it's cheap, but it's also unnecessary. Fix: wrap in useMemo: const displayed = useMemo(() => [...entries].reverse().slice(0, 20), [entries]). Also applies to the filter callback in the parent: entries.filter(e => ...) before passing to ActivityLog — that's also a new array on every App render. Memoize the filtered list with useMemo([activityLog, activityFilter]).

suggestions.title: Vibe streak never increments within a single session
suggestions.description: App.jsx lines 572-580: The vibe streak useEffect has [teamVibe.key] as its dependency. It fires only when teamVibe.key changes to a new value. Within a single browser session, if the team stays at 'crushing' for 10 consecutive 30-second polls, the effect fires ONCE (on first arrival at 'crushing') and then never again — the streak stays at 1. The streak only grows across page reloads with the same vibe, OR if the vibe oscillates (crushing → in-flow → crushing = 2). The tooltip says 'consecutive syncs at this vibe level' which implies it should count polls. Fix: move the streak increment logic into the syncFromGitHub callback, compare teamVibe.key BEFORE and AFTER the fetch, and increment when the key stays the same.

suggestions.title: Inline styles scattered in JSX belong in CSS classes
suggestions.description: App.jsx has multiple inline style blocks that should live in CSS: (1) vibeStreak span: style={{fontSize:'0.72rem',fontWeight:700,color:'var(--accent)',marginLeft:'0.35rem'}} — should be .vibe-streak-badge. (2) worker-info div: style={{ flex: 1, paddingLeft: '0.75rem' }} — should be in .worker-info CSS rule. (3) Section headers: style={{ marginTop: '2.5rem' }} and style={{ fontSize: '0.8rem', color: 'var(--text-muted)' }} — should be .section-header + .section-subtitle. Inline styles bypass the theme system (var() works, but no dark/light overrides possible), break CSS specificity clarity, and can't be overridden by media queries. Small effort, large maintainability payoff.

suggestions.title: Office tab shows empty floor while data loads — no loading state
suggestions.description:
App.jsx: The Dashboard tab shows a LoadingSkeleton during isLoading. The Office tab renders immediately with an empty workers array. On first load the office floor shows only Bloberto, all desks vacant, no characters — which looks broken for ~1-2 seconds before workers arrive. Fix: in the Office tab panel, wrap with {isLoading ?
Loading the team…
: } OR pass isLoading to Office and render a subtle shimmer over the floor. The isSyncing prop is already threaded through — add isLoading alongside it.

suggestions.title: WorkerCard index prop drives CSS stagger animation — unstable on array reorder
suggestions.description: App.jsx: WorkerCard receives index={i} from the .map() call. This index feeds the CSS custom property --i which drives the stagger entrance animation delay. When a worker is added or removed, ALL subsequent workers get a new index value, causing their --i to change and triggering re-animation even for cards that didn't change. More visible when workers drop off (fading). Fix: assign a stable index based on worker.id using a useMemo lookup table (e.g., const stableIndex = useMemo(() => Object.fromEntries(workers.map((w, i) => [w.id, i])), [workers.map(w => w.id).join(',')]) ). Only new workers get a new index; existing workers keep theirs.

suggestions.title: No error boundary — bad JSON crashes the entire app silently
suggestions.description: App.jsx: fetchWorkersFromGitHub and fetchActivityFromGitHub have try/catch for fetch errors (fetchError, fetchWarn state). But if workers.json returns valid JSON with an unexpected shape (e.g., a string instead of array, or a worker missing the 'id' field), runtime errors in WorkerCard, getTeamVibe, or the office desk assignment logic will crash the React tree with a blank white screen. Add an ErrorBoundary component that wraps <App /> in main.jsx, catches render errors, and displays a friendly 'Something went wrong — try refreshing' message with the error detail. React 19 makes this straightforward with the new use() hook patterns or a classic class component boundary.
